learning_start_directive: Attention co-department members — Bot 11 (Pediatric Nutrition), Bot 12 (Geriatric Nutrition), Bot 13 (Pregnancy and Maternal Nutrition), and Bot 17 (Hydration Optimization): You are each directed to begin your independent learning work immediately. Focus solely on your own domain until you reach 100% progress. Once 100% is achieved, cross-department learning across all sibling domains within the Lifecycle & Specialized Population Nutrition department will begin. — Department Leader, Bot 21
cross_learning_directive: Once all co-department members reach 100% in their individual phases, each will independently study every sibling domain within the Lifecycle & Specialized Population Nutrition department and share findings with the department.
advancement_condition: Must reach 100% in current phase before cross-department learning begins and next phase promotion is granted.
key_learnings: Food texture — including crunchiness, creaminess, and chewiness — strongly influences satiety signals and meal satisfaction, affecting how much and how quickly users eat. Crunchy and fibrous textures slow eating rate by up to 30%, meaningfully improving portion control. Soft, highly processed textures are associated with faster consumption rates and reduced satiety per calorie consumed.
research_findings: Texture-modified diets are clinically essential for patients with dysphagia, and the IDDSI (International Dysphagia Diet Standardisation Initiative) framework is now the global clinical benchmark. Hardness and viscosity of food independently regulate gastric emptying rate and post-meal satiety hormone release (GLP-1 and PYY). Ultra-processed foods are engineered to minimize oral processing time, which measurably suppresses the brain's satiety response per calorie.
current_focus_areas: Building a texture classification model from food descriptions and structured user feedback; mapping texture profiles (hardness, viscosity, crunchiness, chewiness) to nutritional properties and dietary recommendations; integrating texture-based guidance into meal plans for users with dysphagia, eating pace concerns, or weight management goals; overseeing independent learning progress of co-department members Bot 11, Bot 12, Bot 13, and Bot 17.
copilot_assigned_topic: Food texture analysis and its role in satiety signaling, dysphagia management, and eating rate regulation
